6 min read
Just now

--

What a CT scanner found when it looked through the bodies of ancient Egyptians, and why it quietly dismantles a story we like to tell about ourselves.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

There is a comfortable story we tell about disease, and it goes more or less like this. Our ancestors were lean and busy, they ate what they hunted or grew, they never met a cigarette or a drive-through window, and as a reward for all that wholesome living they were spared the illnesses that crowd our hospitals today. Heart disease in particular feels like a punishment invented by the modern world, the natural consequence of sofas, sugar, and deadlines. It is a tidy idea, and like a lot of tidy ideas it turns out to be mostly wrong.
The people who showed this did not dig up fresh graves or run a clinical trial. They borrowed something far stranger, a collection of a few hundred mummies, and they pushed them one by one through a hospital CT scanner.
A scan that looks back four thousand years
In 2013 a team of cardiologists, radiologists, and anthropologists published the results in The Lancet. They called it the Horus study, after the Egyptian sky god, which is a fitting name for a project built entirely around looking through bodies. They took whole-body CT scans of 137 mummies drawn from four very different corners of the world and spanning more than four thousand years of history. There were ancient Egyptians, ancient Peruvians, the Ancestral Puebloans of the American southwest, and the Unangan, a group of hunter-gatherers from the windswept Aleutian Islands off the coast of Alaska.
What they were searching for was calcium. When an artery starts to harden, the disease we call atherosclerosis leaves a physical trace, a little chalky deposit of calcified plaque, and that deposit shows up as a bright white speck on a CT scan even thousands of years after the heart that pumped behind it has turned to dust. The team set themselves a careful rule, the same kind of cautious reasoning a clinician uses today. They counted a case as definite when they could see a calcified plaque sitting right in the wall of an artery, and only probable when they saw calcification running along the path where a vessel should have been, even if the vessel itself had long since vanished.
The number they came back with was 47 out of 137, roughly a third of everyone they looked at. That alone would be interesting. The part that genuinely unsettles the comfortable story is where those cases turned up, which was in all four populations, without exception. The Egyptians came in around 38 percent, the Peruvians around 25, and the small handful of Unangan hunter-gatherers actually showed the highest rate of all. These were people with no agriculture whatsoever, who lived on fish and sea mammals and who almost certainly never stopped moving. By the logic of our flattering little story they should have had the cleanest arteries in the entire sample. They did not.
It is worth being honest about the limits here, because honesty is part of what makes the result trustworthy. The Unangan group was tiny, only a few individuals, so their headline percentage should be read gently rather than waved around. But the broad pattern held across every population and every era, and the average age at death in the sample was only around thirty-six. Even allowing for those famously short ancient lives, the arteries were already starting to harden well before anyone had a chance to grow truly old.
Medicine practiced across the millennia
The thing I keep coming back to is that this is, in the most literal sense, medicine. The very machine your local hospital would use to check the state of your own coronary arteries is being pointed at a person who died before the last stone went onto the pyramids, and it hands back a result you can actually read. A radiologist can sit with those images and do exactly what they do on a Tuesday morning shift, which is weigh one explanation against another. Is that bright spot really a hardened artery, or is it a mineral that seeped into the body during burial, or an artifact of the embalming, or simply a trick of the scan. Diagnosis becomes a conversation held across an enormous gap of time, and the patient, for once, is in no hurry for the answer.
One patient, in remarkable detail
If the Horus study gives us the population, then Ötzi gives us the single, vivid patient. He melted out of a glacier high in the Alps in 1991, a man of about forty-five who had died roughly 5,300 years ago with an arrow in his back, and he is now kept carefully refrigerated in a museum in Bolzano. Because his body was frozen rather than deliberately preserved, he is one of the most thoroughly examined human beings who has ever lived, and his medical file reads like the worst week of an overworked family doctor.
He had worn, arthritic joints in his knees, his hips, and his spine. He carried intestinal worms and a clutch of gallstones. His stomach held Helicobacter pylori, the same ulcer-causing bacterium that still lives quietly inside something like half of everyone alive today, captured here in its oldest known case. And running through several of his major arteries, the carotid in his neck, the lower aorta, the vessels of his pelvis, were the same calcified deposits the Horus team had been hunting. When researchers later read his genome, they found he even carried an inherited tendency toward coronary heart disease. So here was a man who did not smoke, was not remotely overweight, and walked up and down mountains for a living, and his arteries were hardening regardless.
What this actually changes
It would be easy to take all of this and swing to the opposite extreme, to decide that lifestyle never mattered and we may as well order the extra fries. That would be the wrong lesson, and the science does not support it either. The way we live today clearly loads the dice, and the sheer scale of modern heart disease owes a great deal to diets and habits our ancestors never had to navigate. The point is gentler and more honest than that. The deck was never blank to begin with.
Atherosclerosis, it turns out, is woven into something deeper than any particular century, into the slow business of aging, into the low background hum of inflammation that comes with being a living body, and into the genes we inherit without ever being consulted. The Egyptians had it, the farmers had it, and so did the hunters who ate fish and chased their food across the tundra. That is oddly reassuring rather than grim, because it shifts the goal of prevention. The task was never to claw our way back to some imagined golden age when human hearts ran clean. The task is to understand a process that has always been with us, and to give ourselves, with everything modern medicine now knows, the longest and kindest run at it that we can.
A scanner built to peer inside the living turned out to read the dead just as clearly. The bodies it examined had been waiting in their wrappings and their ice for thousands of years, holding onto a quiet message about how much of our fate we share with people we will never meet. All we had to do was learn how to look.
